abstract: This paper evaluates X's Community Notes system, which uses a bridging algorithm to select moderation notes that garner cross-partisan support along an inferred latent ideological dimension. The authors analyze the full corpus of 1.9 million moderation notes and 135 million ratings through March 2025, cross-referenced with ideological scaling data across 13 countries. They argue that the system's design systematically undermoderates polarizing content, creating risks for electoral integrity worldwide.
key_claims: Community Notes' bridging-based selection mechanism structurally undermoderates polarizing content by design.
This undermoderation creates concrete risks in electoral processes across diverse political contexts.
The system's performance varies with the polarization context of the country in which it operates.
methods: Large-scale analysis of the complete Community Notes dataset: 1.9 million notes and 135 million ratings up to March 2025.
Cross-referencing note and rating data with ideological scaling data covering 13 countries.
Comparative evaluation of moderation outcomes across polarization contexts.
findings: Notes addressing highly polarizing content are less likely to achieve the cross-partisan agreement required for public display.
The bridging algorithm's behavior differs across national contexts depending on levels of ideological polarization.
Electoral-related content is among the categories most affected by this systematic undermoderation (as implied by the abstract's framing).
contributions: Provides the first large-scale, cross-national empirical evaluation of Community Notes as a global moderation system.
Identifies a structural, design-level limitation of bridging-based moderation rather than a purely operational shortcoming.
Links platform moderation design choices to electoral risk, informing debates on platform governance and democratic integrity.
framing: The paper situates itself in the literature on platform content moderation, algorithmic governance, and bridging-based systems, engaging with debates on how cross-partisan consensus mechanisms perform under polarization. It contributes to research on the democratic implications of social media moderation, particularly regarding elections.
